comments: The first 15 minutes of the show are missing. The last 10 minutes of the show are missing.

sessions.details: Recorded 1980-10-15, first TX. No known commercial release.
archive session recorded on 26th April, 1965, with a line-up including John McVie and Eric Clapton, and first broadcast on Saturday Club on the Light Programme
archive session recorded on 23rd January, 1967, with McVie, Peter Green and Aynsley Dunbar, and first broadcast on Saturday Club on the Light Programme

track_listing.track: He’s a Woman - She’s a Man
Riding on the L and N

transcript.text: This is Tommy Vance on Radio 1.
Never stopped to wonder why
Never learned the town
Didn't wanna be around
Take me down
Life is just a slow train
Crawling up a hill
For the archives
put together in the studios
of the BBC in April 1965,
that was John Mayall,
and as I said,
to the best of our knowledge,
Mr. Clapton was on the guitar.
Three tracks you heard,
the first one was
Crawling Up a Hill,
the second, Crocodile Walk,
and then last one
was Bye Bye Bird,
and there's a great version
of that song,
Bye Bye Bird on the Moody Blues first LP.
Also recorded, I think, around about 1965,
back in the days when it incorporated Mr. Denny Lane.
More from John Mayall later.
Saxon are going to be on Rock on Saturday tomorrow.
They're also on the Friday Rock Show.
And that's something that band could record
the telephone directory of any town or city in the world,
and I'd like it.
They're such a good band.
Whoever's in the band.
Because they change every once in a while, don't they?
Have us hawk wind on a track from their new album.
The album's called Levitation.
The track was Motorway City.
Played a bit of Hawkwind for loads of people,
including Mark and Robin and Tony and Adrian and Ian and Simon and Philip and Joanne
and Kate and Claire and Liz and Jane and everybody else who went to the Isle of Wight camp.
Also, Toby, Windy, Pete, Amish, Mobbin.
Can't read the next one.
It could be Phyllis or Phoebe.
And all the other cranium crushers of William Parker, Upper Sixth in Hastings.
Thank you, says Gonzo and Rudolph the Red-Nosed Werewolf.
Also, Hawkwind was for Baz and Fraz and Arthur.
And all the good mates who go to the YF in Cheadle.
Good wishes for Nigel Fleming, who lives, what do you believe, in Cheadle?
Hawken was also for a lady by the name of Elizabeth McCall, a good friend of John Cooper.
John lives in Glasgow.
Hawtwin, too, was for Hugh and for Goyne, or possibly Gwyn and for Phil,
and for Paul and Mark and Ian and Alistair.
I sound like a telephone director.
And Dave and Wendy and Nick and Moy and Adam from Brecon.
And finally, for everybody who goes to Dunsmore School in rugby.
And before that, you heard something from the new album by Motor.
I beg your pardon, by Saxon.
The album's called Strong Arm of the Law.
The track was Taking Your Chances,
and that was for all the good friends of Mr David Crook,
whom I'm absolutely sure isn't.
It's just about 16 minutes to 11.
Here's a new band you've probably never heard of before.
They were formed in 1975 in Sunderland.
They now live in London.
They've been here for three years.
Give them a chance.
They're called Hibiscus.
Bit of a classic by Rush from the album Hemispheres, The Trees.
For all the Rush freaks around the country,
and good heavens, aren't there a lot of them,
including Tony Hughes and everybody at the Green Garage in Abertillery.
And Abertillery, isn't it?
Well, you know where it is, in Wales.
For Dave and Rich and Mark and Ray from Chelmsley Wood.
And for Andy and all his friends who live in Scotland.
And lots of love to Tracy from Neil Reid.
I'll also for Bod and Fletch and Mike and Matty the Welshman and Stakes the Hare, I think it is.
They all go to the Kingswood School in Bath.
And before that, the first of three from a new band by the name of Hibiscus.
The track you heard was called White Night.
Four minutes to eleven, it's time for the Friday Night Connection.
The last one on the air was a fortnight ago.
We played you the Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse track by Aphrodite's Child.
Then we played you a track from Quadrophenia.
And we played you something from Sab's Volume 4.
Just about everybody got the answer right.
In fact, I think something like 99.999% recurring.
The answer, of course, was four.
Four Horsemen, Quadrovina, and Sabs Volume 4.
But the person that gets the £10 record token is Mr. Phil Saunders,
who lives at number 19, Woodcombe, St. Giles Estate, Whitchurch in Bristol.
So, Phil, you get yourself a £10 record token.
That will be in the post just as soon as I can raise the money out of Tony Wilson.
You know how hard it is, and it gets progressively more difficult as we stay on the air.
and you've put together a really tasty Friday Night Connection yourself.
And here it is, three bits of music within which there is a theme.
And that's our Friday Night Connection for tonight.
It is, of course, three bits of music with a theme or connection contained therein,
intertwined within.
Put together by Mr. Phil Saunders of 19 Woodcombe St. Giles Estate Woodchurch in Bristol,
if you think you know what it is.
And it's pretty, pretty simple, really.
Jot down your answer on a postcard, letter,
any form of communication you like, and send it to
The Friday Night Connection,
The Friday Rock Show, BBC Radio 1,
London, W1A, 4WW.
And if yours is the first card we pull out of the bag,
the one that we find most attractive,
with, of course, the correct answer,
then you'll get yourself a £10 record token.
The best of luck.
The Friday Rock Show from BBC Radio 1.
It's 11 minutes after 11.
Sorry about the lack of personal mentions tonight
for all the people that I met at the Birmingham football match last Sunday.
Unfortunately, I left the bits of paper at home on a shelf,
but I'll dig them out and play them next week.
Meanwhile, for all of you, here's Hibiscus and I See the Sun.
I'm in Southampton on the 20th, or rather I should say the 2nd of October in this year.
That, of course, is the live version of Mr Crowley by Ozzy Osbourne's Blizzard of Ours,
and it was released today, and I played it specially today for a gentleman who had a birthday yesterday.
I think he was 20, and he's feeling a bit depressed
because he thinks he's past it all.
I doubt it very much.
His name is Duncan.
Best wishes from his sister.
His sister's name is Pauline, and they live in Croydon in Surrey.
And prior to that, you heard Hibiscus,
and a track which is called I See the Sun.
Where?
...from an album that came out in 1978,
the album called Taken by Force.
The track was He's a Woman, She's a Man.
And that was for a gentleman by the name of Robin,
Morrison Smith
It seems
It sums him up
Beautifully
But I didn't write that
Somebody else did
It's rather anonymous
But obviously
Somebody who knows him well
Also played the Scorpions
For Mark Jarvis
And all his mates
Who live in Amberley
In Gloucestershire
And prior to that
You heard Thin Lizzy
Live and Dangerous
Was the album
The track was Emerald
And that was for
Stan and Alice
And Coxie and Smithy
And Ian and Gav
And everybody else
Best wishes from G Hilton
Also very best wishes
To a load of people
Who are at the moment
involved in the 36-hour table tennis marathon
in Rishton in Blackburn.
They're all members of the Rishton Scout Group.
That's Adam and Timmy and Martin and Carl and Michael.
Keep on batting that ball.
It's 26 and a half minutes to midnight.
Back into the archives.
Now we go back to 1967 for more John Mayall.
According to John Mayall,
if you were listening to the light program
as it was in those days in, what, January 1967,
You would have heard that the first time it went out.
Three numbers by John Mayall.
Leapin' Christine was the last one.
Before that, Sittin' in the Rain.
And prior to that, Riding on the L and N.
John Anderson's got a new album out.
Here's a track from it.
Absolute magic.
From the new double album that's just come out called Eagles Live, The Eagles There,
and a live version of Hotel California.
And before that, something from the brand new album by John Anderson.
It's a good one.
It's called Song of Seven.
The track was For You, For Me.
And now we're going to play our last from Bob Corner,
who plays the keyboards, Graham Close on the guitar,
Kevin Moore on the bass, and Pete Saunders on the drums.
The four gentlemen who came down from Sunderland some three years ago
down to London, and they're trying to get a recording deal
and also a publishing deal.
So if anybody's interested, after they hear this,
well, write to us and we'll let you know where to find Hibiscus.
This is called Quilter.
